---
title: "Elite Fractures, Public Capture: The Strategic Use of Public Consultation in Global Constitution-Making"
date: 2025-01-10
author:
  - name: Matthew Martin
    url: https://mattjamesmartin.github.io/
    orcid: 0000-0003-3460-3603
    affiliation: Department of Government, University of Texas at Austin
categories:
  - Constitutional design
  - Public consultation
  - Political elites
pub-info:
  year: "2025"
  venue: "Journal of Law and Courts"
  venueDetail: "13(1): 1–34"
  blurb: >-
    Since 1974, two out of every five constitutions (40.3%) were prepared via processes that included public consultation. The reasons for adopting these participatory mechanisms, however, are largely unexplored. I argue that public consultation is a tool for elite contestation of power. Using an original dataset of public consultations in constitution-making processes from 1974–2021 (n = 300), I find that in democracies, factional majorities and newcomer elites use public consultation to legitimate a break from the status quo. In autocracies, governing coalitions that depend on performance and enjoy greater party institutionalization push for public consultation to preserve favorable power-sharing arrangements.
  links:
  - name: Final version
    url: https://doi.org/10.1017/jlc.2024.9
    icon: fa-solid fa-arrow-up-right-from-square
  - name: Data
    url: "https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/PVTH3Z"
    icon: fa-solid fa-database
  extra: >-
    Presented at American Political Science Association (2024), Law and Society      Association (2024), and Northern Political Science Association (2023).
doi: 10.1017/jlc.2024.9
---

## Abstract

Since 1974, two out of every five constitutions (40.3%) were prepared via processes that included public consultation. The reasons for adopting these participatory mechanisms, however, are largely unexplored. I argue that public consultation is a tool for elite contestation of power. Introducing an original dataset of public consultations in constitution-making processes from 1974-2021 (n = 300), I find that in democracies, factional majorities and newcomer elites use public consultation to legitimate a break from the status quo. In autocracies, governing coalitions that depend on performance and enjoy greater party institutionalization push for public consultation to preserve favorable power-sharing arrangements. {{< fa blank >}}

## Key figures

![Figure 2: World Map of Public Consultation in Constitution-Making (1974-2021)](figures/map.png)

```{=html}
<div class="va-figure-pair">
  <figure>
    <img src="figures/full_plot_ord.png" alt="Figure 3a: Predicted Probability of Public Consultation Across Level of Democracy (Lexical Index of Democracy)">
    <figcaption>Figure 3a: Predicted Probability of Public Consultation Across Level of Democracy (Lexical Index of Democracy)</figcaption>
  </figure>
  <figure>
    <img src="figures/full_plot_cont.png" alt="Figure 3b: Predicted Probability of Public Consultation Across Level of Democracy (V-Dem Electoral Democracy Index)">
    <figcaption>Figure 3b: Predicted Probability of Public Consultation Across Level of Democracy (V-Dem Electoral Democracy Index)</figcaption>
  </figure>
</div>
```
